title: Sudden Blindness in Dogs: Causes, Symptoms, and Emergency Care
meta_desc: If your dog has suddenly lost their sight, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care and a photo of their eyes are vital.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden blindness in dogs is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention to diagnose the cause and potentially save your pet's sight. Contact an emergency vet right away and provide a photo of your dog's eyes to help the clinical team prepare for your arrival.

What is sudden onset blindness in dogs and what causes it?

Sudden onset blindness, also known as acute vision loss, occurs when a dog unexpectedly loses the ability to see in one or both eyes. This can happen over a few minutes or a few days. Common causes include Sudden Acquired Retinal Degeneration Syndrome (SARDS), acute glaucoma, retinal detachment, or neurological issues. Because dogs are very good at compensating for gradual vision loss, it may sometimes appear sudden even if it has been progressing slowly, but any perceived sudden change is a cause for immediate concern.

Is sudden blindness in my dog considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Sudden blindness is considered a veterinary emergency that requires immediate attention.
  • Pain and Systemic Health: Many underlying causes are either intensely painful or indicative of a severe systemic health problem.
  • Critical Time Window: For conditions like acute glaucoma or inflammatory diseases, there is a narrow window of time—often just hours—where treatment might be able to restore or save your dog's remaining vision.

How does sending a photo of my dog's eyes help the veterinary triage team?

  • Visual Assessment: A clear, well-lit photo allows the veterinarian to check for visible abnormalities such as pupil dilation, cloudiness, redness, or hemorrhage.
  • Prioritization: This visual information helps the medical staff prioritize your pet's care (triage) based on the severity of the symptoms.
  • Preparation: Sending a photo allows the team to prepare the necessary diagnostic tools and treatment area before you even walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Sudden blindness in dogs can be caused by several conditions including sudden acute retinal degeneration syndrome (SARDS), optic neuritis, retinal detachment, and intracranial disease affecting the optic chiasm, tracts, or occipital cortex. Clinical signs often include acute-onset blindness, dilated pupils, and diminished or absent pupillary light reflexes (PLR). Etiologies of sudden blindness vary, with SARDS potentially linked to unknown causes, increased alkaline phosphatase, ALT, and cholesterol, and optic neuritis potentially associated with systemic diseases such as canine distemper or fungal infections like cryptococcosis and blastomycosis. For acute vision loss, large areas of the retina or optic nerve must be involved. Ophthalmic emergencies require rapid diagnosis and aggressive therapy to maintain vision.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: What are the first signs of sudden blindness?
faqs.answer: Common signs include bumping into furniture, hesitation at stairs, dilated pupils that do not shrink in bright light, and an increased startle reflex when touched.

faqs.question: Can sudden blindness in dogs be cured?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends entirely on the cause. While conditions like SARDS are currently irreversible, vision loss due to high blood pressure or certain inflammatory conditions can sometimes be restored with rapid medical intervention.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog confined in a crate or a small area of the car to prevent them from injuring themselves. Speak in a calm, soothing voice to reduce their anxiety, as losing their sight can be very frightening.
